intro: Prophecy (Hebrew nevuah; Greek propheteia) in the biblical sense encompasses two related functions: forth-telling (declaring God's word to a present situation) and fore-telling (announcing future events). Biblical prophecy is distinguished from pagan divination by its source — not human technique or demonic spirits but the Spirit of God moving upon the prophet (2 Pet. 1:21). The Old Testament contains an extensive system of predictive prophecy, with some 300 specific predictions concerning the Messiah fulfilled in Jesus Christ: his birth in Bethlehem (Mic. 5:2), his entry into Jerusalem on a donkey (Zech. 9:9), the betrayal for thirty pieces of silver (Zech. 11:12–13), and his resurrection (Ps. 16:10). The New Testament identifies prophecy as a spiritual gift given to the church for its edification (1 Cor. 14:3–5), alongside the canonical completion of Scripture.
